steps.title: Identify the key behavioral signal
steps.content: The critical clue is that sunflower sea stars selected the nutritionally poor urchin in 37.5% of trials—nearly as often as the nutritionally rich urchin at 42.7%. This matters because the passage establishes that poor urchins are unappealing to most predators.

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content:
D's phrase “do not always avoid” nutritionally poor urchins directly matches the sea stars' behavior: they feed on poor urchins at substantial rates (37.5%), unlike most predators.

Because sea stars that can eat poor urchins from barrens would help control their expansion if populations recover, their recovery becomes a valuable management tool.

A invents unmeasured prey preferences, B misreads a 37.5% selection rate as reluctance, and C compares sea star species without testing.

That matches choice D—D is correct.
